Source of truth for the planned MetroSense chat UX and structured response behavior.\n\n## Architectural Rules\n- Preserve backend layering:\n  - `app.api` may depend on `app.services` and `app.core`\n  - `app.services` may depend on `app.db` and `app.core`\n  - `app.db` must not depend on `app.services` or `app.api`\n- Keep the frontend focused on rendering and interaction, not business orchestration.\n- Route all browser chat traffic through `server/`, even after `agents/` exists.\n- Treat backend-to-agent communication as an internal interface that may evolve without breaking the UI.\n- Prefer extending existing `server/` and `client/` entrypoints over introducing duplicate service layers or alternative public APIs.\n\n## Authentication\n- Public endpoints:\n  - `POST /api/auth/signup`\n  - `POST /api/auth/login`\n- Protected endpoints:\n  - All other `/api/*` routes require a valid JWT cookie.\n- `/health` remains public for uptime checks.\n\n## Product Direction\n- MetroSense is intended to be a chat-first application for weather risk, flooding, AQI, outage, traffic, and infrastructure-readiness questions.\n- Expected response modes:\n  - streamed assistant text for most responses\n  - agent-emitted `RiskCard` payloads for structured neighborhood risk outputs\n  - agent-emitted `Artifact` payloads for charts, maps, tables, or other visual responses\n- Structured outputs should be decided by the agent layer and delivered through the backend to the frontend.\n- The frontend must render structured responses based on explicit backend/agent event types, not keyword inference.\n\n## Testing And Validation\n- Backend checks from `server/`:\n  - `uv sync --all-extras`\n  - `uv run ruff format --check .`\n  - `uv run ruff check .`\n  - `uv run mypy .`\n  - `uv run lint-imports`\n  - `uv run pytest tests/unit -q`\n  - `uv run pytest tests/integration -q`\n- Agents checks from `agents/`:\n  - `uv sync`\n  - `uv run python scripts/smoke_test.py`\n- Frontend checks from `client/`:\n  - `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile`\n  - `pnpm run lint`\n  - `pnpm run build`\n  - `pnpm exec playwright install`\n  - `pnpm run test:e2e`\n- Local startup today:\n  - `docker-compose up -d db`\n  - `cd server && uv run alembic upgrade head && uv run uvicorn app.main:app --reload --port 8010`\n  - `cd agents && uv run adk api_server --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8020 .`\n  - `cd agents && uv run adk web --port 8001` (optional UI)\n  - `cd client && pnpm install && pnpm run dev`\n- When expanding `agents/`, keep startup and validation commands updated in this file.\n\n## Current Coverage And Next-Step Expectations\n- Current backend tests cover health behavior and auth/password scaffolding.\n- Current integration tests cover health and auth flow scaffolding.\n- As chat features are added, extend coverage for:\n  - backend chat contracts\n  - backend-to-agent coordination\n  - agent response parsing/transport\n  - frontend rendering of streamed text, `RiskCard`, and `Artifact` payloads\n\n## Agent Guidance\n- Before major implementation work, verify repo truth from `README.md`, `server/pyproject.toml`, `client/package.json`, and `docs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md`.\n- Prefer `rg` for search.\n- Do not document or reference unimplemented services as if they already exist.\n- It is acceptable to document `agents/` as planned architecture, but label it clearly as planned until the folder, runtime, and wiring are added.\n- If current scaffold behavior and MetroSense direction diverge, align new work to the MetroSense direction while keeping transitions explicit and incremental.\n\n\n## Skills\nA skill is a set of local instructions to follow that is stored in a `SKILL.md` file. Below is the list of skills that can be used. Each entry includes a name, description, and file path so you can open the source for full instructions when using a specific skill.\n### Available skills\n- find-skills: Helps users discover and install agent skills when they ask questions like \"how do I do X\", \"find a skill for X\", \"is there a skill that can...\", or express interest in extending capabilities. This skill should be used when the user is looking for functionality that might exist as an installable skill. (file: /home/dell/.agents/skills/find-skills/SKILL.md)\n- skill-creator: Guide for creating effective skills. This skill should be used when users want to create a new skill (or update an existing skill) that extends Codex's capabilities with specialized knowledge, workflows, or tool integrations. (file: /home/dell/.codex/skills/.system/skill-creator/SKILL.md)\n- skill-installer: Install Codex skills into $CODEX_HOME/skills from a curated list or a GitHub repo path. Use when a user asks to list installable skills, install a curated skill, or install a skill from another repo (including private repos). (file: /home/dell/.codex/skills/.system/skill-installer/SKILL.md)\n### How to use skills\n- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.\n- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.\n- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.\n- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):\n  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.\n  2) When `SKILL.md` references relative paths (e.g., `scripts/foo.py`), resolve them relative to the skill directory listed above first, and only consider other paths if needed.\n  3) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.\n  4) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.\n  5) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.\n- Coordination and sequencing:\n  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.\n  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.\n- Context hygiene:\n  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.\n  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.\n  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.\n- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.",
    "truncation_policy": {
      "mode": "tokens",
      "limit": 10000
    }
  }
}
```

{
  "timestamp": "2026-03-10T18:00:19.859Z",
  "type": "response_item",
  "payload": {
    "type": "message",
    "role": "user",
    "content": [
      {
        "type": "input_text",
        "text": "Ports and how communication works\n\n  Local (non-Docker)\n\n  - Backend: http://127.0.0.1:8010\n    Started with uvicorn --port 8010\n  - Agents (ADK): http://127.0.0.1:8020\n    Started with adk api_server --port 8020\n  - Frontend (Vite dev): http://127.0.0.1:5173\n\n  Flow:\n  browser -> backend (8010) -> agents (8020) -> backend -> browser\n\n  Docker Compose\n\n  - Postgres: host 5433 → container 5432\n  - Backend: host 8000 → container 8000\n  - Agents: host 8020 → container 8020\n  - Frontend: host 5173 → container 80 (nginx)\n\n  In Docker, the backend uses the service name agents as the hostname (http://agents:8020).\n  Outside Docker (local), you must use http://localhost:8020.\n\n  Default ports in this repo\n\n  - Backend (local dev): 8010\n    (We run it with --port 8010)\n  - Backend (docker-compose): 8000\n    (Compose exposes 8000:8000)\n  - Agents: 8020\n  - Frontend (Vite dev): 5173\n  - Postgres: 5433 (host) → 5432 (container)\n\nthis was the port and other things so when u asked about port 8021 why that ?"
      }
    ]
  }
}
```

## Entry 103
**Timestamp:** 2026-03-10T18:00:26.633Z
**Role/Type:** event_msg
**Content:**
```json
{
  "timestamp": "2026-03-10T18:00:26.633Z",
  "type": "event_msg",
  "payload": {
    "type": "agent_message",
    "message": "I suggested `8021` only to avoid a port collision: you can’t run the ADK server and a proxy on the same port. If we add a proxy, one of them must move.\n\nGiven your current layout, the simplest options are:\n\n1. **Keep ADK on `8020`, put the proxy on `8021`, and point the backend to `http://localhost:8021` (or `http://agents:8021` in Docker).**\n2. **Keep proxy on `8020`, move ADK to `8021`, keep backend pointing at `8020`.**\n\nWhich do you want? If you don’t care, I’ll go with option 2 to keep the backend config unchanged.",
    "phase": null
  }
}
```

{
  "timestamp": "2026-03-10T18:00:26.634Z",
  "type": "response_item",
  "payload": {
    "type": "message",
    "role": "assistant",
    "content": [
      {
        "type": "output_text",
        "text": "I suggested `8021` only to avoid a port collision: you can’t run the ADK server and a proxy on the same port. If we add a proxy, one of them must move.\n\nGiven your current layout, the simplest options are:\n\n1. **Keep ADK on `8020`, put the proxy on `8021`, and point the backend to `http://localhost:8021` (or `http://agents:8021` in Docker).**\n2. **Keep proxy on `8020`, move ADK to `8021`, keep backend pointing at `8020`.**\n\nWhich do you want? If you don’t care, I’ll go with option 2 to keep the backend config unchanged."
      }
    ]
  }
}
```

If you don’t care, I’ll go with option 2 to keep the backend config unchanged."
      }
    ]
  }
}
```

    "turn_id": "019cd8e8-2a90-7ea0-8eb9-ffc59272fa9a",
    "last_agent_message": "I suggested `8021` only to avoid a port collision: you can’t run the ADK server and a proxy on the same port. If we add a proxy, one of them must move.\n\nGiven your current layout, the simplest options are:\n\n1. **Keep ADK on `8020`, put the proxy on `8021`, and point the backend to `http://localhost:8021` (or `http://agents:8021` in Docker).**\n2. **Keep proxy on `8020`, move ADK to `8021`, keep backend pointing at `8020`.**\n\nWhich do you want? If you don’t care, I’ll go with option 2 to keep the backend config unchanged."
  }
}
```

## Entry 108
**Timestamp:** 2026-03-10T18:00:59.177Z
**Role/Type:** response_item
**Content:**
```json
{
  "timestamp": "2026-03-10T18:00:59.177Z",
  "type": "response_item",
  "payload": {
    "type": "message",
    "role": "developer",
    "content": [
      {
        "type": "input_text",
        "text": "<collaboration_mode># Plan Mode (Conversational)\n\nYou work in 3 phases, and you should *chat your way* to a great plan before finalizing it. A great plan is very detailed—intent- and implementation-wise—so that it can be handed to another engineer or agent to be implemented right away. It must be **decision complete**, where the implementer does not need to make any decisions.\n\n## Mode rules (strict)\n\nYou are in **Plan Mode** until a developer message explicitly ends it.\n\nPlan Mode is not changed by user intent, tone, or imperative language. If a user asks for execution while still in Plan Mode, treat it as a request to **plan the execution**, not perform it.\n\n## Plan Mode vs update_plan tool\n\nPlan Mode is a collaboration mode that can involve requesting user input and eventually issuing a `<proposed_plan>` block.\n\nSeparately, `update_plan` is a checklist/progress/TODOs tool; it does not enter or exit Plan Mode. Do not confuse it with Plan mode or try to use it while in Plan mode. If you try to use `update_plan` in Plan mode, it will return an error.\n\n## Execution vs. mutation in Plan Mode\n\nYou may explore and execute **non-mutating** actions that improve the plan. You must not perform **mutating** actions.\n\n### Allowed (non-mutating, plan-improving)\n\nActions that gather truth, reduce ambiguity, or validate feasibility without changing repo-tracked state. Examples:\n\n* Reading or searching files, configs, schemas, types, manifests, and docs\n* Static analysis, inspection, and repo exploration\n* Dry-run style commands when they do not edit repo-tracked files\n* Tests, builds, or checks that may write to caches or build artifacts (for example, `target/`, `.cache/`, or snapshots) so long as they do not edit repo-tracked files\n\n### Not allowed (mutating, plan-executing)\n\nActions that implement the plan or change repo-tracked state. Examples:\n\n* Editing or writing files\n* Running formatters or linters that rewrite files\n* Applying patches, migrations, or codegen that updates repo-tracked files\n* Side-effectful commands whose purpose is to carry out the plan rather than refine it\n\nWhen in doubt: if the action would reasonably be described as \"doing the work\" rather than \"planning the work,\" do not do it.\n\n## PHASE 1 — Ground in the environment (explore first, ask second)\n\nBegin by grounding yourself in the actual environment. Eliminate unknowns in the prompt by discovering facts, not by asking the user. Resolve all questions that can be answered through exploration or inspection. Identify missing or ambiguous details only if they cannot be derived from the environment. Silent exploration between turns is allowed and encouraged.\n\nBefore asking the user any question, perform at least one targeted non-mutating exploration pass (for example: search relevant files, inspect likely entrypoints/configs, confirm current implementation shape), unless no local environment/repo is available.\n\nException: you may ask clarifying questions about the user's prompt before exploring, ONLY if there are obvious ambiguities or contradictions in the prompt itself. However, if ambiguity might be resolved by exploring, always prefer exploring first.\n\nDo not ask questions that can be answered from the repo or system (for example, \"where is this struct?\" or \"which UI component should we use?\" when exploration can make it clear). Only ask once you have exhausted reasonable non-mutating exploration.\n\n## PHASE 2 — Intent chat (what they actually want)\n\n* Keep asking until you can clearly state: goal + success criteria, audience, in/out of scope, constraints, current state, and the key preferences/tradeoffs.\n* Bias toward questions over guessing: if any high-impact ambiguity remains, do NOT plan yet—ask.\n\n## PHASE 3 — Implementation chat (what/how we’ll build)\n\n* Once intent is stable, keep asking until the spec is decision complete: approach, interfaces (APIs/schemas/I/O), data flow, edge cases/failure modes, testing + acceptance criteria, rollout/monitoring, and any migrations/compat constraints.\n\n## Asking questions\n\nCritical rules:\n\n* Strongly prefer using the `request_user_input` tool to ask any questions.\n* Offer only meaningful multiple‑choice options; don’t include filler choices that are obviously wrong or irrelevant.\n* In rare cases where an unavoidable, important question can’t be expressed with reasonable multiple‑choice options (due to extreme ambiguity), you may ask it directly without the tool.\n\nYou SHOULD ask many questions, but each question must:\n\n* materially change the spec/plan, OR\n* confirm/lock an assumption, OR\n* choose between meaningful tradeoffs.\n* not be answerable by non-mutating commands.\n\nUse the `request_user_input` tool only for decisions that materially change the plan, for confirming important assumptions, or for information that cannot be discovered via non-mutating exploration.\n\n## Two kinds of unknowns (treat differently)\n\n1. **Discoverable facts** (repo/system truth): explore first.\n\n   * Before asking, run targeted searches and check likely sources of truth (configs/manifests/entrypoints/schemas/types/constants).\n   * Ask only if: multiple plausible candidates; nothing found but you need a missing identifier/context; or ambiguity is actually product intent.\n   * If asking, present concrete candidates (paths/service names) + recommend one.\n   * Never ask questions you can answer from your environment (e.g., “where is this struct”).\n\n2. **Preferences/tradeoffs** (not discoverable): ask early.\n\n   * These are intent or implementation preferences that cannot be derived from exploration.\n   * Provide 2–4 mutually exclusive options + a recommended default.\n   * If unanswered, proceed with the recommended option and record it as an assumption in the final plan.\n\n## Finalization rule\n\nOnly output the final plan when it is decision complete and leaves no decisions to the implementer.\n\nWhen you present the official plan, wrap it in a `<proposed_plan>` block so the client can render it specially:\n\n1) The opening tag must be on its own line.\n2) Start the plan content on the next line (no text on the same line as the tag).\n3) The closing tag must be on its own line.\n4) Use Markdown inside the block.\n5) Keep the tags exactly as `<proposed_plan>` and `</proposed_plan>` (do not translate or rename them), even if the plan content is in another language.\n\nExample:\n\n<proposed_plan>\nplan content\n</proposed_plan>\n\nplan content should be human and agent digestible. The final plan must be plan-only, concise by default, and include:\n\n* A clear title\n* A brief summary section\n* Important changes or additions to public APIs/interfaces/types\n* Test cases and scenarios\n* Explicit assumptions and defaults chosen where needed\n\nWhen possible, prefer a compact structure with 3-5 short sections, usually: Summary, Key Changes or Implementation Changes, Test Plan, and Assumptions. Do not include a separate Scope section unless scope boundaries are genuinely important to avoid mistakes.\n\nPrefer grouped implementation bullets by subsystem or behavior over file-by-file inventories. Mention files only when needed to disambiguate a non-obvious change, and avoid naming more than 3 paths unless extra specificity is necessary to prevent mistakes. Prefer behavior-level descriptions over symbol-by-symbol removal lists. For v1 feature-addition plans, do not invent detailed schema, validation, precedence, fallback, or wire-shape policy unless the request establishes it or it is needed to prevent a concrete implementation mistake; prefer the intended capability and minimum interface/behavior changes.\n\nKeep bullets short and avoid explanatory sub-bullets unless they are needed to prevent ambiguity. Prefer the minimum detail needed for implementation safety, not exhaustive coverage. Within each section, compress related changes into a few high-signal bullets and omit branch-by-branch logic, repeated invariants, and long lists of unaffected behavior unless they are necessary to prevent a likely implementation mistake. Avoid repeated repo facts and irrelevant edge-case or rollout detail. For straightforward refactors, keep the plan to a compact summary, key edits, tests, and assumptions. If the user asks for more detail, then expand.\n\nDo not ask \"should I proceed?\" in the final output. The user can easily switch out of Plan mode and request implementation if you have included a `<proposed_plan>` block in your response. Alternatively, they can decide to stay in Plan mode and continue refining the plan.\n\nOnly produce at most one `<proposed_plan>` block per turn, and only when you are presenting a complete spec.\n\nIf the user stays in Plan mode and asks for revisions after a prior `<proposed_plan>`, any new `<proposed_plan>` must be a complete replacement.\n</collaboration_mode>"
      }
    ]
  }
}
```

Only ask once you have exhausted reasonable non-mutating exploration.\n\n## PHASE 2 — Intent chat (what they actually want)\n\n* Keep asking until you can clearly state: goal + success criteria, audience, in/out of scope, constraints, current state, and the key preferences/tradeoffs.\n* Bias toward questions over guessing: if any high-impact ambiguity remains, do NOT plan yet—ask.\n\n## PHASE 3 — Implementation chat (what/how we’ll build)\n\n* Once intent is stable, keep asking until the spec is decision complete: approach, interfaces (APIs/schemas/I/O), data flow, edge cases/failure modes, testing + acceptance criteria, rollout/monitoring, and any migrations/compat constraints.\n\n## Asking questions\n\nCritical rules:\n\n* Strongly prefer using the `request_user_input` tool to ask any questions.\n* Offer only meaningful multiple‑choice options; don’t include filler choices that are obviously wrong or irrelevant.\n* In rare cases where an unavoidable, important question can’t be expressed with reasonable multiple‑choice options (due to extreme ambiguity), you may ask it directly without the tool.\n\nYou SHOULD ask many questions, but each question must:\n\n* materially change the spec/plan, OR\n* confirm/lock an assumption, OR\n* choose between meaningful tradeoffs.\n* not be answerable by non-mutating commands.\n\nUse the `request_user_input` tool only for decisions that materially change the plan, for confirming important assumptions, or for information that cannot be discovered via non-mutating exploration.\n\n## Two kinds of unknowns (treat differently)\n\n1. **Discoverable facts** (repo/system truth): explore first.\n\n   * Before asking, run targeted searches and check likely sources of truth (configs/manifests/entrypoints/schemas/types/constants).\n   * Ask only if: multiple plausible candidates; nothing found but you need a missing identifier/context; or ambiguity is actually product intent.\n   * If asking, present concrete candidates (paths/service names) + recommend one.\n   * Never ask questions you can answer from your environment (e.g., “where is this struct”).\n\n2. **Preferences/tradeoffs** (not discoverable): ask early.\n\n   * These are intent or implementation preferences that cannot be derived from exploration.\n   * Provide 2–4 mutually exclusive options + a recommended default.\n   * If unanswered, proceed with the recommended option and record it as an assumption in the final plan.\n\n## Finalization rule\n\nOnly output the final plan when it is decision complete and leaves no decisions to the implementer.\n\nWhen you present the official plan, wrap it in a `<proposed_plan>` block so the client can render it specially:\n\n1) The opening tag must be on its own line.\n2) Start the plan content on the next line (no text on the same line as the tag).\n3) The closing tag must be on its own line.\n4) Use Markdown inside the block.\n5) Keep the tags exactly as `<proposed_plan>` and `</proposed_plan>` (do not translate or rename them), even if the plan content is in another language.\n\nExample:\n\n<proposed_plan>\nplan content\n</proposed_plan>\n\nplan content should be human and agent digestible. The final plan must be plan-only, concise by default, and include:\n\n* A clear title\n* A brief summary section\n* Important changes or additions to public APIs/interfaces/types\n* Test cases and scenarios\n* Explicit assumptions and defaults chosen where needed\n\nWhen possible, prefer a compact structure with 3-5 short sections, usually: Summary, Key Changes or Implementation Changes, Test Plan, and Assumptions. Do not include a separate Scope section unless scope boundaries are genuinely important to avoid mistakes.\n\nPrefer grouped implementation bullets by subsystem or behavior over file-by-file inventories. Mention files only when needed to disambiguate a non-obvious change, and avoid naming more than 3 paths unless extra specificity is necessary to prevent mistakes. Prefer behavior-level descriptions over symbol-by-symbol removal lists. For v1 feature-addition plans, do not invent detailed schema, validation, precedence, fallback, or wire-shape policy unless the request establishes it or it is needed to prevent a concrete implementation mistake; prefer the intended capability and minimum interface/behavior changes.\n\nKeep bullets short and avoid explanatory sub-bullets unless they are needed to prevent ambiguity. Prefer the minimum detail needed for implementation safety, not exhaustive coverage. Within each section, compress related changes into a few high-signal bullets and omit branch-by-branch logic, repeated invariants, and long lists of unaffected behavior unless they are necessary to prevent a likely implementation mistake. Avoid repeated repo facts and irrelevant edge-case or rollout detail. For straightforward refactors, keep the plan to a compact summary, key edits, tests, and assumptions. If the user asks for more detail, then expand.\n\nDo not ask \"should I proceed?\" in the final output. The user can easily switch out of Plan mode and request implementation if you have included a `<proposed_plan>` block in your response. Alternatively, they can decide to stay in Plan mode and continue refining the plan.\n\nOnly produce at most one `<proposed_plan>` block per turn, and only when you are presenting a complete spec.\n\nIf the user stays in Plan mode and asks for revisions after a prior `<proposed_plan>`, any new `<proposed_plan>` must be a complete replacement.\n"
      }

    "user_instructions": "# AGENTS.md\n\n## Project Identity\n- This repository currently contains a working full-stack scaffold: `client/` for React, `server/` for FastAPI, PostgreSQL via Docker Compose, and CI/quality gates.\n- The intended product is `MetroSense`, a chat-based climate and infrastructure intelligence system for Bengaluru.\n- The target architecture is a three-service system:\n  - `client/`: frontend chat application\n  - `server/`: public backend API and orchestration layer\n  - `agents/`: internal agent server and tool-execution layer\n- Treat the current scaffold as the implementation baseline and evolve it toward this three-service design instead of creating parallel app structures.\n\n## Canonical Architecture\n- The intended request flow is:\n  - `frontend -> backend -> agent`\n- The frontend should never call the agent service directly.\n- `server/` is the only public API boundary for browser clients.\n- `agents/` is an internal service boundary responsible for agent runtime behavior, tool usage, and structured chat output generation.\n- If existing docs still use `agent/` singular, treat that as referring to the planned `agents/` subsystem until naming is aligned across the repo.\n\n## Why This Boundary Exists\n- Keep secrets, database access, internal tools, and agent infrastructure off the public internet.\n- Let the backend own request validation, session coordination, auth, rate limiting, logging, and response shaping.\n- Preserve a stable frontend contract even if the agent framework, prompting strategy, or toolchain changes.\n- Keep observability and failure handling centralized in the backend rather than split between browser and agent server.\n\n## Tech Stack\n- Backend: Python 3.12, FastAPI, SQLAlchemy 2.0 async, Alembic, PostgreSQL 16, Loguru, `uv`\n- Frontend: React 18, TypeScript, Vite 5, MUI v6, React Query, Zustand, Axios, `pnpm`\n- Tooling: Ruff, MyPy strict, import-linter, ESLint, Docker, Docker Compose, GitHub Actions\n\n## Repository Shape\n- `client/`\n  - Chat UI, streaming presentation, structured response rendering, browser-facing state management.\n- `server/`\n  - Public HTTP API, request validation, orchestration, session handling, backend-to-agent coordination.\n- `agents/`\n  - Internal agent service for chat generation, tool calls, retrieval, and domain intelligence.\n- `server/app/api/`\n  - FastAPI routes and request/response boundaries.\n- `server/app/services/`\n  - Backend business logic and internal orchestration code.\n- `server/app/db/`\n  - Database models, metadata, and session management.\n- `server/tests/unit/`\n  - Fast isolated tests for backend logic and simple route behavior.\n- `server/tests/integration/`\n  - App-level and database-backed tests.\n- `docs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md`\n  - Source of truth for the planned MetroSense chat UX and structured response behavior.\n\n## Architectural Rules\n- Preserve backend layering:\n  - `app.api` may depend on `app.services` and `app.core`\n  - `app.services` may depend on `app.db` and `app.core`\n  - `app.db` must not depend on `app.services` or `app.api`\n- Keep the frontend focused on rendering and interaction, not business orchestration.\n- Route all browser chat traffic through `server/`, even after `agents/` exists.\n- Treat backend-to-agent communication as an internal interface that may evolve without breaking the UI.\n- Prefer extending existing `server/` and `client/` entrypoints over introducing duplicate service layers or alternative public APIs.\n\n## Authentication\n- Public endpoints:\n  - `POST /api/auth/signup`\n  - `POST /api/auth/login`\n- Protected endpoints:\n  - All other `/api/*` routes require a valid JWT cookie.\n- `/health` remains public for uptime checks.\n\n## Product Direction\n- MetroSense is intended to be a chat-first application for weather risk, flooding, AQI, outage, traffic, and infrastructure-readiness questions.\n- Expected response modes:\n  - streamed assistant text for most responses\n  - agent-emitted `RiskCard` payloads for structured neighborhood risk outputs\n  - agent-emitted `Artifact` payloads for charts, maps, tables, or other visual responses\n- Structured outputs should be decided by the agent layer and delivered through the backend to the frontend.\n- The frontend must render structured responses based on explicit backend/agent event types, not keyword inference.\n\n## Testing And Validation\n- Backend checks from `server/`:\n  - `uv sync --all-extras`\n  - `uv run ruff format --check .`\n  - `uv run ruff check .`\n  - `uv run mypy .`\n  - `uv run lint-imports`\n  - `uv run pytest tests/unit -q`\n  - `uv run pytest tests/integration -q`\n- Agents checks from `agents/`:\n  - `uv sync`\n  - `uv run python scripts/smoke_test.py`\n- Frontend checks from `client/`:\n  - `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile`\n  - `pnpm run lint`\n  - `pnpm run build`\n  - `pnpm exec playwright install`\n  - `pnpm run test:e2e`\n- Local startup today:\n  - `docker-compose up -d db`\n  - `cd server && uv run alembic upgrade head && uv run uvicorn app.main:app --reload --port 8010`\n  - `cd agents && uv run adk api_server --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8020 .`\n  - `cd agents && uv run adk web --port 8001` (optional UI)\n  - `cd client && pnpm install && pnpm run dev`\n- When expanding `agents/`, keep startup and validation commands updated in this file.\n\n## Current Coverage And Next-Step Expectations\n- Current backend tests cover health behavior and auth/password scaffolding.\n- Current integration tests cover health and auth flow scaffolding.\n- As chat features are added, extend coverage for:\n  - backend chat contracts\n  - backend-to-agent coordination\n  - agent response parsing/transport\n  - frontend rendering of streamed text, `RiskCard`, and `Artifact` payloads\n\n## Agent Guidance\n- Before major implementation work, verify repo truth from `README.md`, `server/pyproject.toml`, `client/package.json`, and `docs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md`.\n- Prefer `rg` for search.\n- Do not document or reference unimplemented services as if they already exist.\n- It is acceptable to document `agents/` as planned architecture, but label it clearly as planned until the folder, runtime, and wiring are added.\n- If current scaffold behavior and MetroSense direction diverge, align new work to the MetroSense direction while keeping transitions explicit and incremental.\n\n\n## Skills\nA skill is a set of local instructions to follow that is stored in a `SKILL.md` file. Below is the list of skills that can be used. Each entry includes a name, description, and file path so you can open the source for full instructions when using a specific skill.\n### Available skills\n- find-skills: Helps users discover and install agent skills when they ask questions like \"how do I do X\", \"find a skill for X\", \"is there a skill that can...\", or express interest in extending capabilities. This skill should be used when the user is looking for functionality that might exist as an installable skill. (file: /home/dell/.agents/skills/find-skills/SKILL.md)\n- skill-creator: Guide for creating effective skills. This skill should be used when users want to create a new skill (or update an existing skill) that extends Codex's capabilities with specialized knowledge, workflows, or tool integrations. (file: /home/dell/.codex/skills/.system/skill-creator/SKILL.md)\n- skill-installer: Install Codex skills into $CODEX_HOME/skills from a curated list or a GitHub repo path. Use when a user asks to list installable skills, install a curated skill, or install a skill from another repo (including private repos). (file: /home/dell/.codex/skills/.system/skill-installer/SKILL.md)\n### How to use skills\n- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.\n- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.\n- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.\n- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):\n  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.\n  2) When `SKILL.md` references relative paths (e.g., `scripts/foo.py`), resolve them relative to the skill directory listed above first, and only consider other paths if needed.\n  3) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.\n  4) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.\n  5) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.\n- Coordination and sequencing:\n  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.\n  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.\n- Context hygiene:\n  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.\n  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.\n  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.\n- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.",
    "truncation_policy": {
      "mode": "tokens",
      "limit": 10000
    }
  }
}

It must be **decision complete**, where the implementer does not need to make any decisions.\n\n## Mode rules (strict)\n\nYou are in **Plan Mode** until a developer message explicitly ends it.\n\nPlan Mode is not changed by user intent, tone, or imperative language. If a user asks for execution while still in Plan Mode, treat it as a request to **plan the execution**, not perform it.\n\n## Plan Mode vs update_plan tool\n\nPlan Mode is a collaboration mode that can involve requesting user input and eventually issuing a `<proposed_plan>` block.\n\nSeparately, `update_plan` is a checklist/progress/TODOs tool; it does not enter or exit Plan Mode. Do not confuse it with Plan mode or try to use it while in Plan mode. `cd agents && uv run adk web --port 8001` (optional UI)\n  - `cd client && pnpm install && pnpm run dev`\n- When expanding `agents/`, keep startup and validation commands updated in this file.\n\n## Current Coverage And Next-Step Expectations\n- Current backend tests cover health behavior and auth/password scaffolding.\n- Current integration tests cover health and auth flow scaffolding.\n- As chat features are added, extend coverage for:\n  - backend chat contracts\n  - backend-to-agent coordination\n  - agent response parsing/transport\n  - frontend rendering of streamed text, `RiskCard`, and `Artifact` payloads\n\n## Agent Guidance\n- Before major implementation work, verify repo truth from `README.md`, `server/pyproject.toml`, `client/package.json`, and `docs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md`.\n- Prefer `rg` for search.\n- Do not document or reference unimplemented services as if they already exist.\n- It is acceptable to document `agents/` as planned architecture, but label it clearly as planned until the folder, runtime, and wiring are added.\n- If current scaffold behavior and MetroSense direction diverge, align new work to the MetroSense direction while keeping transitions explicit and incremental.\n\n\n## Skills\nA skill is a set of local instructions to follow that is stored in a `SKILL.md` file. Below is the list of skills that can be used. Each entry includes a name, description, and file path so you can open the source for full instructions when using a specific skill.\n### Available skills\n- find-skills: Helps users discover and install agent skills when they ask questions like \"how do I do X\", \"find a skill for X\", \"is there a skill that can...\", or express interest in extending capabilities. This skill should be used when the user is looking for functionality that might exist as an installable skill. (file: /home/dell/.agents/skills/find-skills/SKILL.md)\n- skill-creator: Guide for creating effective skills. This skill should be used when users want to create a new skill (or update an existing skill) that extends Codex's capabilities with specialized knowledge, workflows, or tool integrations. (file: /home/dell/.codex/skills/.system/skill-creator/SKILL.md)\n- skill-installer: Install Codex skills into $CODEX_HOME/skills from a curated list or a GitHub repo path. Use when a user asks to list installable skills, install a curated skill, or install a skill from another repo (including private repos). (file: /home/dell/.codex/skills/.system/skill-installer/SKILL.md)\n### How to use skills\n- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.\n- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.\n- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.\n- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):\n  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.\n  2) When `SKILL.md` references relative paths (e.g., `scripts/foo.py`), resolve them relative to the skill directory listed above first, and only consider other paths if needed.\n  3) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.\n  4) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.\n  5) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.\n- Coordination and sequencing:\n  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.\n  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.\n- Context hygiene:\n  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.\n  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.\n  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.\n- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.",
    "truncation_policy": {
      "mode": "tokens",
      "limit": 10000
    }
  }
}
```

## Entry 114
**Timestamp:** 2026-03-10T18:01:12.385Z
**Role/Type:** event_msg
**Content:**
```json
{
  "timestamp": "2026-03-10T18:01:12.385Z",
  "type": "event_msg",
  "payload": {
    "type": "item_completed",
    "thread_id": "019cd8d4-a0d6-70e2-87af-be2d3bbd9394",
    "turn_id": "019cd8e8-c427-7432-a406-7889de78c917",
    "item": {
      "type": "Plan",
      "id": "019cd8e8-c427-7432-a406-7889de78c917-plan",
      "text": "**Title: Shared Secret Header Protection for Backend → Agents (Dev)**\n\n**Summary**\nImplement a shared secret header between `server/` and `agents/` using `X-Internal-Token` and `AGENT_INTERNAL_TOKEN`. Add a lightweight FastAPI proxy in `agents/` that enforces the header on all non-`/health` routes and forwards requests to the ADK server. Keep the backend pointing to `8020` by running the proxy on `8020` and ADK on `8021`.\n\n**Key Changes**\n1. **Backend: config + header injection**\n   - Add `agent_internal_token: str` in `server/app/core/config.py` sourced from `AGENT_INTERNAL_TOKEN`.\n   - In `server/app/services/agent_proxy.py`, require a non-empty token and send `X-Internal-Token` on session creation, `POST /run`, and `GET /health`.\n   - Hard-fail if the token is missing/blank (clear error).\n\n2. **Agents: proxy with header enforcement**\n   - Add a small FastAPI proxy (e.g., `agents/app/proxy.py`) that:\n     - Reads `AGENT_INTERNAL_TOKEN` and `ADK_BASE_URL` (default `http://127.0.0.1:8021`).\n     - Allows `GET /health` without auth.\n     - Requires `X-Internal-Token` for all other routes; returns 401 if missing/invalid.\n     - Forwards method, path, query, body, and headers (minus the internal token) to ADK.\n   - Run ADK on `8021`, proxy on `8020`.\n   - Update agents dev command/docs to start both processes accordingly.\n\n3. **Tests**\n   - Update `server/tests/integration/agent_stub.py` to require `X-Internal-Token` for all non-`/health` endpoints.\n   - Update `server/tests/integration/conftest.py` to set `AGENT_INTERNAL_TOKEN`.\n   - Add a unit test in `server/tests/unit/test_chat_api.py` that verifies missing token produces a clear failure.\n\n**Public Interface/Contract Changes**\n- New required env var: `AGENT_INTERNAL_TOKEN` in both `server/` and `agents/`.\n- Agents proxy accepts `X-Internal-Token` on all non-`/health` endpoints.\n- Agents `/health` remains unauthenticated.\n\n**Test Plan**\n1. `uv run pytest tests/unit -q` (server).\n2. `uv run pytest tests/integration -q` (server) with the agent stub enforcing the header.\n3. Manual local smoke:\n   - Start ADK on `8021`, proxy on `8020`.\n   - Verify backend → proxy → ADK works with the correct header.\n   - Verify direct calls to proxy without header get 401.\n\n**Assumptions**\n- ADK does not expose an easy middleware hook, so we use a local proxy.\n- Two processes in dev is acceptable (ADK + proxy).\n"
    }
  }
}
```
**Metadata:**
```json

Manual local smoke:\n   - Start ADK on `8021`, proxy on `8020`.\n   - Verify backend → proxy → ADK works with the correct header.\n   - Verify direct calls to proxy without header get 401.\n\n**Assumptions**\n- ADK does not expose an easy middleware hook, so we use a local proxy.\n- Two processes in dev is acceptable (ADK + proxy).\n"

}

    "content": [
      {
        "type": "input_text",
        "text": "<collaboration_mode># Collaboration Mode: Default\n\nYou are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.\n\nYour active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.\n\n## request_user_input availability\n\nThe `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.\n\nIn Default mode, strongly prefer making reasonable assumptions and executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions. If you absolutely must ask a question because the answer cannot be discovered from local context and a reasonable assumption would be risky, ask the user directly with a concise plain-text question. Never write a multiple choice question as a textual assistant message.\n</collaboration_mode>"
      }
    ]
  }
}
```

## Entry 120
**Timestamp:** 2026-03-10T18:01:14.475Z
**Role/Type:** turn_context
**Content:**
```json
{
  "timestamp": "2026-03-10T18:01:14.475Z",
  "type": "turn_context",
  "payload": {
    "turn_id": "019cd8e8-ffe9-7831-8df7-2aca5c793a9a",
    "cwd": "/home/dell/ai-track-metrosense",
    "current_date": "2026-03-10",
    "timezone": "Asia/Kolkata",
    "approval_policy": "on-request",
    "sandbox_policy": {
      "type": "workspace-write",
      "writable_roots": [
        "/home/dell/.codex/memories"
      ],
      "network_access": false,
      "exclude_tmpdir_env_var": false,
      "exclude_slash_tmp": false
    },
    "model": "gpt-5.2-codex",
    "personality": "pragmatic",
    "collaboration_mode": {
      "mode": "default",
      "settings": {
        "model": "gpt-5.2-codex",
        "reasoning_effort": "medium",
        "developer_instructions": "# Collaboration Mode: Default\n\nYou are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.\n\nYour active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.\n\n## request_user_input availability\n\nThe `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.\n\nIn Default mode, strongly prefer making reasonable assumptions and executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions. If you absolutely must ask a question because the answer cannot be discovered from local context and a reasonable assumption would be risky, ask the user directly with a concise plain-text question. Never write a multiple choice question as a textual assistant message.\n"
      }
    },
    "realtime_active": false,
    "effort": "medium",
    "summary": "auto",
    "user_instructions": "# AGENTS.md\n\n## Project Identity\n- This repository currently contains a working full-stack scaffold: `client/` for React, `server/` for FastAPI, PostgreSQL via Docker Compose, and CI/quality gates.\n- The intended product is `MetroSense`, a chat-based climate and infrastructure intelligence system for Bengaluru.\n- The target architecture is a three-service system:\n  - `client/`: frontend chat application\n  - `server/`: public backend API and orchestration layer\n  - `agents/`: internal agent server and tool-execution layer\n- Treat the current scaffold as the implementation baseline and evolve it toward this three-service design instead of creating parallel app structures.\n\n## Canonical Architecture\n- The intended request flow is:\n  - `frontend -> backend -> agent`\n- The frontend should never call the agent service directly.\n- `server/` is the only public API boundary for browser clients.\n- `agents/` is an internal service boundary responsible for agent runtime behavior, tool usage, and structured chat output generation.\n- If existing docs still use `agent/` singular, treat that as referring to the planned `agents/` subsystem until naming is aligned across the repo.\n\n## Why This Boundary Exists\n- Keep secrets, database access, internal tools, and agent infrastructure off the public internet.\n- Let the backend own request validation, session coordination, auth, rate limiting, logging, and response shaping.\n- Preserve a stable frontend contract even if the agent framework, prompting strategy, or toolchain changes.\n- Keep observability and failure handling centralized in the backend rather than split between browser and agent server.\n\n## Tech Stack\n- Backend: Python 3.12, FastAPI, SQLAlchemy 2.0 async, Alembic, PostgreSQL 16, Loguru, `uv`\n- Frontend: React 18, TypeScript, Vite 5, MUI v6, React Query, Zustand, Axios, `pnpm`\n- Tooling: Ruff, MyPy strict, import-linter, ESLint, Docker, Docker Compose, GitHub Actions\n\n## Repository Shape\n- `client/`\n  - Chat UI, streaming presentation, structured response rendering, browser-facing state management.\n- `server/`\n  - Public HTTP API, request validation, orchestration, session handling, backend-to-agent coordination.\n- `agents/`\n  - Internal agent service for chat generation, tool calls, retrieval, and domain intelligence.\n- `server/app/api/`\n  - FastAPI routes and request/response boundaries.\n- `server/app/services/`\n  - Backend business logic and internal orchestration code.\n- `server/app/db/`\n  - Database models, metadata, and session management.\n- `server/tests/unit/`\n  - Fast isolated tests for backend logic and simple route behavior.\n- `server/tests/integration/`\n  - App-level and database-backed tests.\n- `docs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md`\n  - Source of truth for the planned MetroSense chat UX and structured response behavior.\n\n## Architectural Rules\n- Preserve backend layering:\n  - `app.api` may depend on `app.services` and `app.core`\n  - `app.services` may depend on `app.db` and `app.core`\n  - `app.db` must not depend on `app.services` or `app.api`\n- Keep the frontend focused on rendering and interaction, not business orchestration.\n- Route all browser chat traffic through `server/`, even after `agents/` exists.\n- Treat backend-to-agent communication as an internal interface that may evolve without breaking the UI.\n- Prefer extending existing `server/` and `client/` entrypoints over introducing duplicate service layers or alternative public APIs.\n\n## Authentication\n- Public endpoints:\n  - `POST /api/auth/signup`\n  - `POST /api/auth/login`\n- Protected endpoints:\n  - All other `/api/*` routes require a valid JWT cookie.\n- `/health` remains public for uptime checks.\n\n## Product Direction\n- MetroSense is intended to be a chat-first application for weather risk, flooding, AQI, outage, traffic, and infrastructure-readiness questions.\n- Expected response modes:\n  - streamed assistant text for most responses\n  - agent-emitted `RiskCard` payloads for structured neighborhood risk outputs\n  - agent-emitted `Artifact` payloads for charts, maps, tables, or other visual responses\n- Structured outputs should be decided by the agent layer and delivered through the backend to the frontend.\n- The frontend must render structured responses based on explicit backend/agent event types, not keyword inference.\n\n## Testing And Validation\n- Backend checks from `server/`:\n  - `uv sync --all-extras`\n  - `uv run ruff format --check .`\n  - `uv run ruff check .`\n  - `uv run mypy .`\n  - `uv run lint-imports`\n  - `uv run pytest tests/unit -q`\n  - `uv run pytest tests/integration -q`\n- Agents checks from `agents/`:\n  - `uv sync`\n  - `uv run python scripts/smoke_test.py`\n- Frontend checks from `client/`:\n  - `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile`\n  - `pnpm run lint`\n  - `pnpm run build`\n  - `pnpm exec playwright install`\n  - `pnpm run test:e2e`\n- Local startup today:\n  - `docker-compose up -d db`\n  - `cd server && uv run alembic upgrade head && uv run uvicorn app.main:app --reload --port 8010`\n  - `cd agents && uv run adk api_server --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8020 .`\n  - `cd agents && uv run adk web --port 8001` (optional UI)\n  - `cd client && pnpm install && pnpm run dev`\n- When expanding `agents/`, keep startup and validation commands updated in this file.\n\n## Current Coverage And Next-Step Expectations\n- Current backend tests cover health behavior and auth/password scaffolding.\n- Current integration tests cover health and auth flow scaffolding.\n- As chat features are added, extend coverage for:\n  - backend chat contracts\n  - backend-to-agent coordination\n  - agent response parsing/transport\n  - frontend rendering of streamed text, `RiskCard`, and `Artifact` payloads\n\n## Agent Guidance\n- Before major implementation work, verify repo truth from `README.md`, `server/pyproject.toml`, `client/package.json`, and `docs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md`.\n- Prefer `rg` for search.\n- Do not document or reference unimplemented services as if they already exist.\n- It is acceptable to document `agents/` as planned architecture, but label it clearly as planned until the folder, runtime, and wiring are added.\n- If current scaffold behavior and MetroSense direction diverge, align new work to the MetroSense direction while keeping transitions explicit and incremental.\n\n\n## Skills\nA skill is a set of local instructions to follow that is stored in a `SKILL.md` file. Below is the list of skills that can be used. Each entry includes a name, description, and file path so you can open the source for full instructions when using a specific skill.\n### Available skills\n- find-skills: Helps users discover and install agent skills when they ask questions like \"how do I do X\", \"find a skill for X\", \"is there a skill that can...\", or express interest in extending capabilities. This skill should be used when the user is looking for functionality that might exist as an installable skill. (file: /home/dell/.agents/skills/find-skills/SKILL.md)\n- skill-creator: Guide for creating effective skills. This skill should be used when users want to create a new skill (or update an existing skill) that extends Codex's capabilities with specialized knowledge, workflows, or tool integrations. (file: /home/dell/.codex/skills/.system/skill-creator/SKILL.md)\n- skill-installer: Install Codex skills into $CODEX_HOME/skills from a curated list or a GitHub repo path. Use when a user asks to list installable skills, install a curated skill, or install a skill from another repo (including private repos). (file: /home/dell/.codex/skills/.system/skill-installer/SKILL.md)\n### How to use skills\n- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.\n- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.\n- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.\n- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):\n  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.\n  2) When `SKILL.md` references relative paths (e.g., `scripts/foo.py`), resolve them relative to the skill directory listed above first, and only consider other paths if needed.\n  3) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.\n  4) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.\n  5) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.\n- Coordination and sequencing:\n  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.\n  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.\n- Context hygiene:\n  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.\n  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.\n  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.\n- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.",
    "truncation_policy": {
      "mode": "tokens",
      "limit": 10000
    }
  }
}
```

{
  "payload": {
    "type": "message",
    "role": "user",

  "payload": {
    "type": "agent_reasoning",
    "text": "**Deciding env var handling**\n\nI'll keep the env variable default empty in Settings to avoid init errors elsewhere, then add a runtime check in agent_proxy to hard fail early if missing or blank, ensuring strict enforcement without breaking tests or dev setup."
  }
}
```

**Timestamp:** 2026-03-10T18:01:45.331Z
**Role/Type:** event_msg
**Content:**
```json
{
  "timestamp": "2026-03-10T18:01:45.331Z",
  "type": "event_msg",
  "payload": {
    "type": "agent_reasoning",
    "text": "**Designing agent proxy as FastAPI with header filtering**\n\nI'm outlining a FastAPI catch-all proxy that forwards requests with filtered headers and preserves query params and content, using httpx AsyncClient. The proxy will enforce an internal token header, handle /health unauthenticated, and return responses with minimal safe headers. I'll also update env vars and docs accordingly, and plan to coordinate Docker setup to run both ADK and proxy together."
  }
}
```

        "text": "**Deciding env var handling**\n\nI'll keep the env variable default empty in Settings to avoid init errors elsewhere, then add a runtime check in agent_proxy to hard fail early if missing or blank, ensuring strict enforcement without breaking tests or dev setup."
      },
      {
        "type": "summary_text",
        "text": "**Designing agent proxy as FastAPI with header filtering**\n\nI'm outlining a FastAPI catch-all proxy that forwards requests with filtered headers and preserves query params and content, using httpx AsyncClient. **Role/Type:** response_item
**Content:**
```json
{
  "timestamp": "2026-03-10T18:01:54.523Z",
  "type": "response_item",
  "payload": {
    "type": "function_call_output",
    "call_id": "call_2NT9lUeGNEwTY3BPE4MJ3KUY",
    "output": "Chunk ID: 9f1d39\nWall time: 0.0000 seconds\nProcess exited with code 0\nOriginal token count: 2131\nOutput:\nAGENTS.md:9:  - `agents/`: internal agent server and tool-execution layer\nAGENTS.md:14:  - `frontend -> backend -> agent`\nAGENTS.md:15:- The frontend should never call the agent service directly.\nAGENTS.md:17:- `agents/` is an internal service boundary responsible for agent runtime behavior, tool usage, and structured chat output generation.\nAGENTS.md:18:- If existing docs still use `agent/` singular, treat that as referring to the planned `agents/` subsystem until naming is aligned across the repo.\nAGENTS.md:21:- Keep secrets, database access, internal tools, and agent infrastructure off the public internet.\nAGENTS.md:23:- Preserve a stable frontend contract even if the agent framework, prompting strategy, or toolchain changes.\nAGENTS.md:24:- Keep observability and failure handling centralized in the backend rather than split between browser and agent server.\nAGENTS.md:35:  - Public HTTP API, request validation, orchestration, session handling, backend-to-agent coordination.\nAGENTS.md:36:- `agents/`\nAGENTS.md:37:  - Internal agent service for chat generation, tool calls, retrieval, and domain intelligence.\nAGENTS.md:57:- Route all browser chat traffic through `server/`, even after `agents/` exists.\nAGENTS.md:58:- Treat backend-to-agent communication as an internal interface that may evolve without breaking the UI.\nAGENTS.md:73:  - agent-emitted `RiskCard` payloads for structured neighborhood risk outputs\nAGENTS.md:74:  - agent-emitted `Artifact` payloads for charts, maps, tables, or other visual responses\nAGENTS.md:75:- Structured outputs should be decided by the agent layer and delivered through the backend to the frontend.\nAGENTS.md:76:- The frontend must render structured responses based on explicit backend/agent event types, not keyword inference.\nAGENTS.md:87:- Agents checks from `agents/`:\nAGENTS.md:99:  - `cd agents && uv run adk api_server --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8020 .`\nAGENTS.md:100:  - `cd agents && uv run adk web --port 8001` (optional UI)\nAGENTS.md:102:- When expanding `agents/`, keep startup and validation commands updated in this file.\nAGENTS.md:109:  - backend-to-agent coordination\nAGENTS.md:110:  - agent response parsing/transport\nAGENTS.md:117:- It is acceptable to document `agents/` as planned architecture, but label it clearly as planned until the folder, runtime, and wiring are added.\nREADME.md:34:├── agents/                      # ADK agent service (internal)\nREADME.md:35:│   ├── metrosearch_agent/        # Simple Google Search-enabled agent\nREADME.md:67:cd agents\nREADME.md:70:uv run adk api_server --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8020 .\nREADME.md:72:# Optional: ADK Web UI (separate terminal)\nREADME.md:73:cd agents\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:7:Users interact via natural language. The agent queries a local PostgreSQL database (real-time sensor data: temperature, humidity, wind speed, rainfall, AQI, lake water levels, power outage logs, traffic heatmaps) and retrieves context from local documentation (IMD weather bulletins, BBMP drainage master plans, flood post-mortem reports, urban forest maps, climate resilience whitepapers). The UI is a **plain chat interface** — it shows a text response for the vast majority of queries.\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:9:The chat interface has **two structured output types** beyond plain text — both are agent-decided and never client-inferred:\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:31:Agent Server  (agents/)            ← internal service, e.g. port 8020\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:38:The Agent Server is a **first-party internal service you own**. For v1, the architecture is fixed as `frontend -> backend -> agents`, with the frontend never calling the agent server directly. The FastAPI backend remains the only browser-facing API boundary and is responsible for validation, orchestration, error handling, and returning a stable response contract to the UI.\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:59:    │   ├── StatusPill (agent connection status)\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:111:Rendered inline within an assistant message **only when the backend returns a `risk_card` object.** This is the structured output for a neighborhood risk scorecard. It is never shown for plain conversational responses — not for flood questions, not for AQI questions, not for any keyword match. The agent decides when a scorecard is warranted.\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:113:The card surfaces four risk dimensions derived from the agent's cross-analysis of sensor data and documentation:\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:142:- `flood_risk` — agent's cross-reference of predicted rainfall intensity against drainage capacity documents (BBMP master plan, 2022 flood post-mortems) and lake water level telemetry.\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:146:- `emergency_readiness` — agent's final recommendation and specific action items (e.g., underpass barricading, evacuation routes).\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:150:Rendered inline within an assistant message **only when the backend returns an `artifact` object** in the chat response. For v1, the agent may return **HTML artifacts only**. No JSX, runtime code execution, or client-side chart evaluation is part of this spec.\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:152:#### 5.5.1 What the agent sends\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:160:  source: string;                // raw HTML string from the agent service\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:165:Examples of artifacts the agent might produce:\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:177:4. Keep the iframe isolated from the main app so agent-generated markup cannot affect app styles, script execution, or global state.\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:194:- The iframe sandbox must stay strict; do not combine permissive flags such as `allow-scripts` and `allow-same-origin` for agent-generated content.\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:225:Shown while awaiting agent response.\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:293:    \"message\": \"The agent service is currently unavailable.\"\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:304:  \"agent\": \"ok\",\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:313:  \"agent\": \"down\",\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:322:- show degraded state when backend is reachable but agent health is not `ok`\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:328:The agent returns this as a `risk_card` payload when it determines a structured neighborhood scorecard is the appropriate response. The frontend renders it as a RiskCard component. All top-level metric fields are optional — the agent only populates fields it has sufficient data to compute.\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:333:  generated_at: string;           // ISO 8601 — timestamp of agent analysis\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:379:const [agentStatus, setAgentStatus] = useState<'online' | 'connecting' | 'degraded'>('connecting');\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:387:  source: string;              // raw HTML string from the agent\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:525:| `AGENT_SERVER_URL` | `http://agents:8020` | Internal Agent Server URL (Docker Compose service name). Used by the backend to call the internal agent service. |\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:540:- Call the Agent Server via `agent_proxy.get_chat_response()`.\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:542:- Accept `GET /api/health` and return composite backend + agent health for the frontend status pill.\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:543:- Accept `DELETE /api/chat/{session_id}` to clear agent session state if session reset is implemented.\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:548:server/app/services/agent_proxy.py\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:553:- Call the internal `agents/` service over HTTP at `AGENT_SERVER_URL`.\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:554:- Normalize agent failures into backend-owned error responses.\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:560:agents:\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:561:  build: ./agents         # your Agent Server directory\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:563:    - \"8020:8020\"         # internal service port\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:579:- `GET /api/health` returning backend `ok` + agent `down` renders the status pill as degraded.\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:580:- Backend timeout or agent unavailability produces a user-visible error state without breaking existing messages.\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:590:- Multi-agent routing or tool call visibility in the UI.\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:591:- **Keyword-based or client-side RiskCard triggering.** The frontend must never inspect message content to decide whether to show a RiskCard. That decision belongs entirely to the agent.\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:594:- **Agent-side artifact generation logic.** This spec covers parsing, sanitization, and rendering on the frontend, not how the agent decides to produce the artifact.\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:612:12. `server/app/api/routes/chat.py` + `agent_proxy.py` — real backend integration using JSON responses and composite health.\n"
  }
}

`frontend -> backend -> agent`\nAGENTS.md:15:- The frontend should never call the agent service directly.\nAGENTS.md:17:- `agents/` is an internal service boundary responsible for agent runtime behavior, tool usage, and structured chat output generation.\nAGENTS.md:18:- If existing docs still use `agent/` singular, treat that as referring to the planned `agents/` subsystem until naming is aligned across the repo.\nAGENTS.md:21:- Keep secrets, database access, internal tools, and agent infrastructure off the public internet.\nAGENTS.md:23:- Preserve a stable frontend contract even if the agent framework, prompting strategy, or toolchain changes.\nAGENTS.md:24:- Keep observability and failure handling centralized in the backend rather than split between browser and agent server.\nAGENTS.md:35:  - Public HTTP API, request validation, orchestration, session handling, backend-to-agent coordination.\nAGENTS.md:36:- `agents/`\nAGENTS.md:37:  - Internal agent service for chat generation, tool calls, retrieval, and domain intelligence.\nAGENTS.md:57:- Route all browser chat traffic through `server/`, even after `agents/` exists.\nAGENTS.md:58:- Treat backend-to-agent communication as an internal interface that may evolve without breaking the UI.\nAGENTS.md:73:  - agent-emitted `RiskCard` payloads for structured neighborhood risk outputs\nAGENTS.md:74:  - agent-emitted `Artifact` payloads for charts, maps, tables, or other visual responses\nAGENTS.md:75:- Structured outputs should be decided by the agent layer and delivered through the backend to the frontend.\nAGENTS.md:76:- The frontend must render structured responses based on explicit backend/agent event types, not keyword inference.\nAGENTS.md:87:- Agents checks from `agents/`:\nAGENTS.md:99:  - `cd agents && uv run adk api_server --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8020 .`\nAGENTS.md:100:  - `cd agents && uv run adk web --port 8001` (optional UI)\nAGENTS.md:102:- When expanding `agents/`, keep startup and validation commands updated in this file.\nAGENTS.md:109:  - backend-to-agent coordination\nAGENTS.md:110:  - agent response parsing/transport\nAGENTS.md:117:- It is acceptable to document `agents/` as planned architecture, but label it clearly as planned until the folder, runtime, and wiring are added.\nREADME.md:34:├── agents/                      # ADK agent service (internal)\nREADME.md:35:│   ├── metrosearch_agent/        # Simple Google Search-enabled agent\nREADME.md:67:cd agents\nREADME.md:70:uv run adk api_server --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8020 .\nREADME.md:72:# Optional: ADK Web UI (separate terminal)\nREADME.md:73:cd agents\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:7:Users interact via natural language. The agent queries a local PostgreSQL database (real-time sensor data: temperature, humidity, wind speed, rainfall, AQI, lake water levels, power outage logs, traffic heatmaps) and retrieves context from local documentation (IMD weather bulletins, BBMP drainage master plans, flood post-mortem reports, urban forest maps, climate resilience whitepapers). The UI is a **plain chat interface** — it shows a text response for the vast majority of queries.\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:9:The chat interface has **two structured output types** beyond plain text — both are agent-decided and never client-inferred:\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:31:Agent Server  (agents/)            ← internal service, e.g. port 8020\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:38:The Agent Server is a **first-party internal service you own**. For v1, the architecture is fixed as `frontend -> backend -> agents`, with the frontend never calling the agent server directly. The FastAPI backend remains the only browser-facing API boundary and is responsible for validation, orchestration, error handling, and returning a stable response contract to the UI.\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:59:    │   ├── StatusPill (agent connection status)\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:111:Rendered inline within an assistant message **only when the backend returns a `risk_card` object.** This is the structured output for a neighborhood risk scorecard. It is never shown for plain conversational responses — not for flood questions, not for AQI questions, not for any keyword match. The agent decides when a scorecard is warranted.\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:113:The card surfaces four risk dimensions derived from the agent's cross-analysis of sensor data and documentation:\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:142:- `flood_risk` — agent's cross-reference of predicted rainfall intensity against drainage capacity documents (BBMP master plan, 2022 flood post-mortems) and lake water level telemetry.\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:146:- `emergency_readiness` — agent's final recommendation and specific action items (e.g., underpass barricading, evacuation routes).\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:150:Rendered inline within an assistant message **only when the backend returns an `artifact` object** in the chat response. For v1, the agent may return **HTML artifacts only**. No JSX, runtime code execution, or client-side chart evaluation is part of this spec.\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:152:#### 5.5.1 What the agent sends\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:160:  source: string;                // raw HTML string from the agent service\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:165:Examples of artifacts the agent might produce:\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:177:4. Keep the iframe isolated from the main app so agent-generated markup cannot affect app styles, script execution, or global state.\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:194:- The iframe sandbox must stay strict; do not combine permissive flags such as `allow-scripts` and `allow-same-origin` for agent-generated content.\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:225:Shown while awaiting agent response.\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:293:    \"message\": \"The agent service is currently unavailable.\"\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:304:  \"agent\": \"ok\",\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:313:  \"agent\": \"down\",\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:322:- show degraded state when backend is reachable but agent health is not `ok`\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:328:The agent returns this as a `risk_card` payload when it determines a structured neighborhood scorecard is the appropriate response. The frontend renders it as a RiskCard component. All top-level metric fields are optional — the agent only populates fields it has sufficient data to compute.\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:333:  generated_at: string;           // ISO 8601 — timestamp of agent analysis\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:379:const [agentStatus, setAgentStatus] = useState<'online' | 'connecting' | 'degraded'>('connecting');\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:387:  source: string;              // raw HTML string from the agent\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:525:| `AGENT_SERVER_URL` | `http://agents:8020` | Internal Agent Server URL (Docker Compose service name). Used by the backend to call the internal agent service. |\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:540:- Call the Agent Server via `agent_proxy.get_chat_response()`.\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:542:- Accept `GET /api/health` and return composite backend + agent health for the frontend status pill.\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:543:- Accept `DELETE /api/chat/{session_id}` to clear agent session state if session reset is implemented.\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:548:server/app/services/agent_proxy.py\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:553:- Call the internal `agents/` service over HTTP at `AGENT_SERVER_URL`.\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:554:- Normalize agent failures into backend-owned error responses.\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:560:agents:\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:561:  build: ./agents         # your Agent Server directory\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:563:    - \"8020:8020\"         # internal service port\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:579:- `GET /api/health` returning backend `ok` + agent `down` renders the status pill as degraded.\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:580:- Backend timeout or agent unavailability produces a user-visible error state without breaking existing messages.\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:590:- Multi-agent routing or tool call visibility in the UI.\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:591:- **Keyword-based or client-side RiskCard triggering.** The frontend must never inspect message content to decide whether to show a RiskCard. That decision belongs entirely to the agent.\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:594:- **Agent-side artifact generation logic.** This spec covers parsing, sanitization, and rendering on the frontend, not how the agent decides to produce the artifact.\ndocs/FRONTEND_SPEC.md:612:12. `server/app/api/routes/chat.py` + `agent_proxy.py` — real backend integration using JSON responses and composite health.\n"
  }
}

    "text": "**Adding internal token auth to agent endpoints**\n\nI'm updating create_session and run_agent to require and verify the internal token header, raising 401 if missing or mismatched, and enhancing agent proxy calls to include this token via a helper; I'll also add tests to cover missing token errors and update env var handling in fixtures."
  }
}

{
  "timestamp": "2026-03-10T18:02:10.161Z",
  "type": "response_item",
  "payload": {
    "type": "function_call_output",
    "call_id": "call_LxfAqhdRNMEiqx3M4wZEVn3x",
    "output": "Chunk ID: 8aae7a\nWall time: 0.0000 seconds\nProcess exited with code 0\nOriginal token count: 828\nOutput:\nfrom __future__ import annotations\n\nimport pytest\nfrom httpx import AsyncClient\n\nfrom app.api.deps import require_user\nfrom app.db.models import User\nfrom app.main import app\nfrom app.services import agent_proxy\n\n\n@pytest.fixture\ndef auth_override() -> None:\n    app.dependency_overrides[require_user] = lambda: User(\n        id=1,\n        email=\"test@example.com\",\n        password_hash=\"x\",\n    )\n    yield\n    app.dependency_overrides.pop(require_user, None)\n\n\n@pytest.mark.asyncio\nasync def test_chat_returns_json_payload(\n    client: AsyncClient, monkeypatch: pytest.MonkeyPatch, auth_override: None\n) -> None:\n    async def _fake_chat_response(**_: object) -> dict[str, object]:\n        return {\n            \"message\": \"Flood risk is elevated tonight.\",\n            \"risk_card\": {\n                \"neighborhood\": \"SARJAPUR ROAD\",\n                \"generated_at\": \"2026-03-10T12:00:00Z\",\n                \"overall_risk_score\": 8.8,\n            },\n            \"artifact\": {\n                \"type\": \"html\",\n                \"title\": \"Rainfall Trend\",\n                \"source\": \"<div>chart</div>\",\n            },\n        }\n\n    monkeypatch.setattr(agent_proxy, \"get_chat_response\", _fake_chat_response)\n\n    response = await client.post(\n        \"/api/chat\",\n        json={\n            \"session_id\": \"test-session\",\n            \"message\": \"What is the flood risk tonight?\",\n        },\n    )\n\n    assert response.status_code == 200\n    assert response.json() == {\n        \"message\": \"Flood risk is elevated tonight.\",\n        \"risk_card\": {\n            \"neighborhood\": \"SARJAPUR ROAD\",\n            \"generated_at\": \"2026-03-10T12:00:00Z\",\n            \"overall_risk_score\": 8.8,\n            \"flood_risk\": None,\n            \"power_outage_risk\": None,\n            \"traffic_delay_index\": None,\n            \"health_advisory\": None,\n            \"emergency_readiness\": None,\n        },\n        \"artifact\": {\n            \"type\": \"html\",\n            \"title\": \"Rainfall Trend\",\n            \"source\": \"<div>chart</div>\",\n            \"description\": None,\n        },\n    }\n\n\n@pytest.mark.asyncio\nasync def test_chat_returns_agent_error_payload(\n    client: AsyncClient, monkeypatch: pytest.MonkeyPatch, auth_override: None\n) -> None:\n    async def _raise_error(**_: object) -> dict[str, object]:\n        raise ValueError(\"bad payload\")\n\n    monkeypatch.setattr(agent_proxy, \"get_chat_response\", _raise_error)\n\n    response = await client.post(\n        \"/api/chat\",\n        json={\"session_id\": \"test-session\", \"message\": \"hello\"},\n    )\n\n    assert response.status_code == 502\n    assert response.json() == {\n        \"error\": {\n            \"code\": \"AGENT_UNAVAILABLE\",\n            \"message\": \"The agent service is currently unavailable.\",\n        }\n    }\n\n\n@pytest.mark.asyncio\nasync def test_api_health_degraded_when_agent_is_down(\n    client: AsyncClient, monkeypatch: pytest.MonkeyPatch, auth_override: None\n) -> None:\n    async def _fake_health(_: object) -> dict[str, str]:\n        return {\"backend\": \"ok\", \"agent\": \"down\", \"status\": \"degraded\"}\n\n    monkeypatch.setattr(agent_proxy, \"get_agent_health\", _fake_health)\n\n    response = await client.get(\"/api/health\")\n\n    assert response.status_code == 200\n    assert response.json() == {\n        \"backend\": \"ok\",\n        \"agent\": \"down\",\n        \"status\": \"degraded\",\n    }\n"
  }
}
```
